systemOverview.description: TaskFlow is a multi-tier web application composed of a React single-page application, a Node.js RESTful API, and a shared TypeScript types package. The system supports real-time collaboration on task management with GitHub/GitLab integration for engineering teams.
systemOverview.productRelationship: Directly implements the product vision of reducing context-switching overhead by 40% through unified task, code review, and deployment tracking. The architecture prioritizes real-time responsiveness, integration extensibility, and multi-tenancy from day one.
technicalConstraints: Must support 1000 concurrent users at launch
OAuth2 required for third-party integrations (GitHub, GitLab)
PostgreSQL and Redis are the chosen persistence technologies
All API endpoints must be versioned under /api/v1
Shared types package must be generated from OpenAPI schema

projectTopology.name: Web Application
projectTopology.purpose: Primary user interface for task management, dashboards, sprint planning, and team collaboration

projectTopology.name: Backend API
projectTopology.purpose: RESTful API handling authentication, business logic, integrations with GitHub/GitLab, and data persistence

projectTopology.name: Shared Types Package
projectTopology.purpose: TypeScript type definitions and API contract types shared between web-app and backend-api

frontends.name: TaskFlow Web App
frontends.description: React SPA with real-time WebSocket updates and optimistic UI rendering
responsibilities: Manage local state with optimistic updates

backends.name: TaskFlow API Server
backends.description: Express.js REST API with WebSocket support for real-time event broadcasting
responsibilities: Authenticate users via JWT with refresh tokens
Broadcast task updates via WebSocket pub/sub

backgroundProcessing.name: Webhook Processor
backgroundProcessing.description: Bull queue worker processing incoming GitHub/GitLab webhook events asynchronously
responsibilities: Parse and validate incoming webhook payloads
Map PR events to task status updates
Retry failed webhook processing with exponential backoff

externalIntegrations.name: GitHub API
externalIntegrations.purpose: PR tracking, commit linking, and webhook event ingestion

externalIntegrations.name: GitLab API
externalIntegrations.purpose: Merge request tracking and pipeline status

externalIntegrations.name: Auth0
externalIntegrations.purpose: OAuth2/OIDC identity provider for user authentication

dataDomains.name: Task Management
dataDomains.description: Tasks, boards, columns, labels, assignees, and comments

dataDomains.name: Sprint Planning
dataDomains.description: Sprints, velocity tracking, capacity planning, and retrospectives

dataDomains.name: Identity & Tenancy
dataDomains.description: Users, organizations, teams, roles, and permissions

dataDomains.name: Integrations
dataDomains.description: Third-party integration configurations, tokens, and event logs

persistenceStrategy.rationale: Strong relational model for multi-tenant task data with JSON support for flexible metadata
Pub/sub for real-time WebSocket broadcasting and caching for session/rate-limiting

boundaries: All data access goes through the backend-api; web-app never connects directly to databases
Organization-level data isolation enforced at query level via tenant_id columns
Integration tokens encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL using application-level encryption

stateOwnership.accessPattern: CRUD via REST API, real-time updates via WebSocket
CRUD via REST API, velocity computed server-side
Local state management with optimistic updates synced to backend

apiBoundaries.name: TaskFlow REST API
apiBoundaries.protocol: REST over HTTPS with JWT auth

internalIntegrationPoints.description: Shared TypeScript types for API request/response contracts
OpenAPI schema used to generate shared-types package

externalServices.name: GitHub API v4 (GraphQL)
externalServices.purpose: Query PR details, commit history, and review status
externalServices.integrationMethod: Server-side GraphQL client with OAuth2 app token

externalServices.name: Auth0 Management API
externalServices.purpose: User provisioning, role management, and organization setup
externalServices.integrationMethod: Server-side REST client with M2M token

externalServices.name: SendGrid
externalServices.purpose: Transactional email for invitations and notifications
externalServices.integrationMethod: Server-side REST API with API key

authAssumptions: Auth0 handles identity, MFA, and session management
JWT tokens with 15-minute access / 7-day refresh rotation
Organization-level RBAC: owner, admin, member, viewer
API endpoints enforce role-based access via middleware
secretHandling: All secrets stored in environment variables, never in code
Integration OAuth tokens encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL
GitHub webhook secrets validated via HMAC-SHA256 signature
Redis connections use TLS in production
trustBoundaries: Web app to backend API: JWT-authenticated HTTPS
Backend API to PostgreSQL: Connection pooling with TLS
Backend API to external services: Server-side only, never exposed to frontend
Webhook ingestion: Signature validation before processing
riskySurfaces: Webhook endpoint is publicly accessible and must validate signatures rigorously
WebSocket connections must re-validate JWT on reconnection
File attachment uploads must be scanned and size-limited
Rate limiting required on auth endpoints to prevent credential stuffing

environmentModel.name: local
environmentModel.purpose: Developer machines with Docker Compose for PostgreSQL and Redis

deploymentUnits.strategy: Static SPA deployed to CDN with immutable cache headers
deploymentUnits.notes: Vercel or Cloudflare Pages for automatic preview deployments

deploymentUnits.strategy: Containerized Node.js deployed to managed container service
deploymentUnits.notes: Auto-scaling based on CPU/memory; health check on /api/v1/health

deploymentUnits.strategy: Published to private npm registry on version bump
deploymentUnits.notes: CI publishes on merge to main when version changes in package.json

qualityAttributes.maintainability: Strict TypeScript with shared types ensures contract safety across projects. Modular architecture with clear boundaries enables independent team velocity.
qualityAttributes.scalability: Stateless API servers behind a load balancer scale horizontally. Redis pub/sub decouples WebSocket broadcasting from request handling. Database read replicas available for reporting queries.
qualityAttributes.testability: Shared types enable contract testing. Backend has unit, integration, and API tests. Frontend has component and E2E tests. Webhook processing testable via Bull queue mocking.
qualityAttributes.reliability: Database transactions for data consistency. Webhook processing with retry queues. Circuit breaker pattern for external API calls. Health checks and graceful shutdown.
qualityAttributes.performance: P95 API latency target under 200ms. WebSocket event propagation under 500ms. CDN-served frontend with code splitting. Database query optimization with indexes on tenant_id and foreign keys.
qualityAttributes.developerExperience: Mono-repo tooling with shared lint/format configs. Docker Compose for one-command local setup. OpenAPI-generated types eliminate manual sync. Preview deployments for every PR.

architectureSlices: backend-api: Auth, task CRUD, sprint management
web-app: Task board, sprint views, real-time updates
GitHub integration: Webhook handler and PR sync
API integration tests against real PostgreSQL
E2E tests for critical user flows
Performance load testing for 1000 concurrent users

rules: All API endpoints must validate input with Zod schemas before processing
Every database query must include tenant_id in WHERE clauses for multi-tenancy isolation
WebSocket messages must follow the shared event schema defined in shared-types
All external API calls must use the circuit breaker wrapper
Error responses must follow RFC 7807 Problem Details format
boundariesToPreserve: web-app must never import from backend-api directly; use shared-types for contracts
Database access is exclusively through backend-api; no direct DB connections from other projects
External service credentials must never be exposed to the frontend
Webhook processing must be asynchronous via the Bull queue, never synchronous in the request handler
antiPatterns: Do not store session state in the API server process; use Redis or JWT
Do not bypass the shared-types package by duplicating type definitions
Do not use raw SQL without parameterized queries
Do not add blocking I/O in WebSocket message handlers
Do not commit .env files or secrets to version control
codingExpectations: TypeScript strict mode enabled in all projects
100% of public API functions must have JSDoc documentation
Test coverage minimum: 80% for business logic, 60% for UI components
All PRs require at least one approval before merge
Conventional Commits format for all commit messages

openRisks.description: WebSocket connection scaling beyond 1000 concurrent users may require dedicated infrastructure (e.g., Socket.io with Redis adapter or dedicated WebSocket gateway)
openRisks.mitigation: Design WebSocket layer as a pluggable adapter so it can be swapped from in-process to dedicated gateway without changing business logic

openRisks.description: GitHub API rate limits (5000 requests/hour for OAuth apps) may be insufficient for organizations with many active PRs
openRisks.mitigation: Implement intelligent caching and webhook-driven updates to minimize polling; use GraphQL to reduce request count

openRisks.description: Multi-tenancy data isolation relies on application-level enforcement; a missing tenant_id filter could leak data across organizations
openRisks.mitigation: Implement tenant-scoped database middleware that automatically injects tenant_id into all queries; add integration tests that verify isolation

openRisks.description: Hard dependency on Auth0 for authentication; outage would prevent all user logins
openRisks.mitigation: JWT validation is local after initial login; implement graceful degradation that allows existing sessions to continue during Auth0 outages

openQuestions.question: Should GitLab integration be included in MVP or deferred to post-launch?
openQuestions.context: Adding GitLab doubles the integration surface area and webhook handling complexity

openQuestions.question: Should the API be designed with mobile clients in mind from the start?
openQuestions.context: Mobile app is currently deferred to v2 but API design decisions now could make mobile integration easier later

openQuestions.question: What is the preferred file storage service for task attachments?
openQuestions.context: Options include S3, Cloudflare R2, or GCS; choice affects deployment and cost model

openQuestions.question: Is SAML/SSO required for beta launch or can it be added post-launch?
openQuestions.context: Enterprise customers typically require SSO but it adds significant implementation effort to the auth flow
